turns.student: How have you been feeling overall lately?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been feeling pretty terrible. The crampy abdominal pain and bloody stools are really worrisome, and the constant diarrhea is making it hard for me to sleep and function at work.

turns.student: Tell me about your day-to-day — how has your health been in general?
turns.patient: It's been a nightmare. I've had 8-10 bowel movements per day, which is just ridiculous, and most of them are loose or watery, with urgency that's hard to control. The fatigue from it all is affecting my work performance, and I'm just exhausted all the time.

turns.student: You mentioned some health issues. Can you walk me through what happens?
turns.patient: I have this crampy lower abdominal pain, predominantly on the left side, which comes and goes but never really subsides. Sometimes it's accompanied by bloody stools with mucus, bright red blood mixed with stool, which is just terrifying to see, you know?

turns.student: And when these episodes occur, what does your body do?
turns.patient: The pain gets worse and feels like a tight knot in my stomach that's hard to breathe around. During those episodes, I get this intense urge to have a bowel movement, which is always urgent and loose or watery, leaving me feeling incomplete even after I've gone.

turns.student: Let's focus on the episodes. Walk me through one from start to finish.
turns.patient: Okay, so it starts with this crampy pain in my lower abdomen, usually on the left side, which gets progressively worse and feels like a tight knot that's hard to breathe around. Then, I'll start feeling this intense urge to have a bowel movement, which is always urgent and loose or watery. When I do go, it's accompanied by bloody stools with mucus, bright red blood mixed with stool, which is just really scary to see. Afterward, I'm left feeling incomplete, like there's still something inside me that needs to come out, even though I've already had a bowel movement – that's what they call tenesmus, right?

turns.student: During an episode, do you notice anything besides the main symptom?
turns.patient: Yes, during episodes, I also experience fatigue affecting my work performance, which worsens with the diarrhea. It's like my body is just drained of all energy and I feel like I'm running on empty, even after resting or trying to take breaks throughout the day.

turns.student: Specifically, any changes in your skin — like sweating or flushing?
turns.patient: No, actually, I haven't noticed any significant changes in my skin.

turns.student: And your stomach — any queasiness or nausea during these moments?
turns.patient: Actually, yes. During some of the episodes, I do experience a bit of queasiness or nausea, especially when I see the bloody stools with mucus – it's really unsettling to look at and can make me feel quite lightheaded.

turns.student: What about other parts of your body? Any pain, tingling, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: Actually, yes. Besides the abdominal pain, I've also been experiencing achy pain in both knees and ankles, no swelling or redness, which seems to be worsening with the diarrhea and my overall condition. It's quite annoying and makes everyday activities even more challenging for me.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: Yes, I've been experiencing low-grade fever (37.8-38.0°C), a few times per week, which is really concerning and adds to my overall sense of unease about what's going on with my body. The fevers are not severe, but they're frequent enough that it's hard for me to shake the feeling that something more serious is brewing beneath the surface.

turns.student: Now let's zoom out. Any swelling you've noticed anywhere on your body?
turns.patient: Actually, besides the knees and ankles, I haven't noticed any significant swelling on my body. The achy pain in those areas seems to be a constant companion, but it's not accompanied by any noticeable swelling or redness.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your weight or appetite?
turns.patient: Yes, I've actually experienced unintentional weight loss of about 4 kg (9 lbs) over the past three weeks. My appetite has been irregular due to my long work hours and mostly taking out food, but even when I do eat, I don't feel like I'm retaining much nutrition, which is concerning.

turns.student: Let me ask about your medical history. What conditions have you been treated for?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've had an appendectomy at age 16, and that was a pretty straightforward surgery with no complications. Besides that, I don't have any significant prior medical conditions to speak of, which is why this current situation is so unsettling for me – it feels like something entirely new and unexpected is happening in my body.

turns.student: Any medications you're taking? Are you taking them consistently?
turns.patient: I've been self-medicating with loperamide (Imodium) for diarrhea for the past 2 weeks, but it's only provided minimal relief. I also take ibuprofen 2-3 times per week for the joint pain in my knees and ankles, which seems to worsen the abdominal symptoms when taken together with Imodium.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle. How active are you?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been feeling really unwell lately, so my activity level has decreased significantly. I used to enjoy going for walks or jogs in the evenings after work, but now even just walking to the kitchen feels like a chore due to the fatigue and discomfort from my symptoms. I've also had to cut back on social activities with friends and family because it's hard for me to keep up with anything that requires too much energy.

turns.student: And your diet — what does a typical day of eating look like?
turns.patient: My diet has been pretty irregular lately due to my long work hours. I mostly end up taking out food or grabbing something quick, which isn't ideal for maintaining regular bowel movements or managing my symptoms. I've also noticed that when I do eat, it's hard for me to retain much nutrition, which is concerning given the weight loss and other symptoms I'm experiencing.

turns.student: How about alcohol or smoking?
turns.patient: I actually drink 2-3 beers most weeknights, but over the past month, I've increased it to 4-5 per night due to stress from my symptoms and work. And no, I'm not a smoker at all.

turns.student: Now family history. Any medical problems run in your family?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was around 30 years old. It's been tough for me to see her go through that, and I've always wondered if there might be a genetic component to it, given our shared family background.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious or chronic conditions?
turns.patient: Yes, my older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. It's been tough for me to see her go through that, and I've always wondered if there might be a genetic component to it, given our shared family background. My father also has type 2 diabetes, but my mother is generally healthy.

turns.student: We've covered a lot. Is there anything we haven't discussed that you think is relevant?
turns.patient:
To be honest, I'm still worried about how this could all be connected – the crampy pain, the bloody stools, the diarrhea, and the fatigue. It feels like everything is linked somehow, but I just can't figure out what's causing it all to start with. And then there's my sister's Crohn's disease... I keep wondering if maybe something similar could be going on in my body too, even though the doctors haven't suggested that yet.

Also, I do want to mention that I've been working 60+ hour weeks at a startup as a software engineer, and the stress from work has definitely increased my beer consumption over the past month. I'm not sure if that's relevant or not, but it feels like everything in my life is connected somehow – my job, my health, my family history...
